title: Double-click on login submit button does not dispatch two authentication requests causing duplicate session creation or account lockout acceleration
description: A user double-clicking the submit button (common on slow connections or desktop habits) must not fire two simultaneous authentication API calls. Duplicate calls can cause race conditions in session creation, prematurely consume rate-limit or account-lockout counters, or create two active sessions. The button must be disabled or the handler must be idempotent from the first click onward.
expected_result: PASS: Exactly one authentication API request is fired; the submit button is disabled from the moment of first click until the response is received; only one session is created; rate-limit counters are debited once. FAIL: Two POST requests are logged to the auth endpoint, two session tokens are issued, account lockout counter is decremented twice, or the UI navigates twice causing a double redirect error.

test_steps.action: Apply Slow 3G network throttle (400–600ms latency) via browser devtools or test framework network conditions
test_steps.expected: Network throttle is active before the page is loaded
Email field contains the test account email

test_steps.action: Fill in the password field with the correct password
Double-click the submit button rapidly (simulate impatient user)
test_steps.expected: First click fires the API request; button immediately becomes disabled or unresponsive to further clicks

test_steps.action: Click the submit button a second time immediately after the first (within 200ms)
test_steps.expected: Second click is ignored because the button is disabled or the handler guards against re-entry

test_steps.action: Wait for authentication to complete and the success redirect
test_steps.expected: User is redirected to the dashboard exactly once; only one session token is issued

test_steps.action: Verify only one network request was dispatched to the auth endpoint (check network log count)
test_steps.expected: Network log shows exactly 1 POST to the authentication endpoint, not 2

test_steps.action: Teardown: Log out to clear the created session
test_steps.expected: User is logged out and returned to the login page or home page
